IN THE HIGH COURT OF LESOTHO
(Commercial Court Division)
HELD AT MASERU                                                            CCT/0010/25
In the matter between:
TUMO TLELAI                                                                       PLAINTIFF
AND
THOLO ENERGY (PTY) LTD                                              DEFENDANT
Neutral Citation:
Tumo Tlelai v. Tholo Energy (Pty) Ltd
[2026] LSHC 40
COM (24
th
February 2026)
CORUM:             MATHABA J
Heard on:              24
th
November 2025
Delivered on:       24
th
February 2026
SUMMARY
Arbitration – Applicant instituting application on an issue that is arbitrable in circumstances where dispute resolution is regulated by arbitration agreement –Courts should respect the sanctity of contracts and be slow to intervene unless agreements offend public policy – A party to arbitration agreement does not necessarily have to invoke the remedy of stay under section 7 of the Arbitration Act 1980 to object to jurisdiction – Jurisdiction declined.
ANNOTATIONS
STATUTES
Arbitration
Act No.12 of 1980
High Court Civil Litigation Rules No. 46 of 2024
CASES
LESOTHO
Bataung Chabeli Construction (PTY) LTD v. Road Fund and others (C of A (CIV) 34 of 2020) 2021 LSCA 17 (14
th
May 2021)
Kompi v. Government of Lesotho (C of A No 43B/2021)
[2022] LSCA 12
(13
th
May 2022)
Mantaote Patience Ntaote v. Principal Secretary Ministry of Finance and Development Planning & Others C of A (CIV) No. 55/2024
Phomolong Investment (Pty) Ltd v. KEL Property Company (Pty) Ltd C of A (CIV) 28/2022)
[2022] LSCA 50
(11 November 2022)
RULING
Introduction
[1]     The issue for determination in this ruling is the sustainability of a preliminary issue regarding lack of jurisdiction and
lis pendens
raised by the respondent to the applicant’s originating application.
Background facts
[2]     The facts are not complicated. It is not necessary to traverse them in detail. On 29 July 2020 the applicant and the respondent concluded a sub-lease agreement in terms of which the respondent was sublet a filling station on plot numbers 13302-019 and 13302-020. The agreement was registered and became effective on 7 September 2022. The dispute has arisen between the parties. The applicant alleges that the respondent breached the sub-lease agreement in that it failed to make advance rent payment in the sum of M6, 693,718.33 and monthly rentals. The applicant is therefore claiming the advance rent payment and arrear rentals.
[3]     In response to the originating application, the respondent raised a preliminary issue regarding this Court’s jurisdiction to hear the matter as well as the defence of
lis pendens.
The parties’ contentions
[4]     The respondent contends that the matter is not justiciable before this Court in that the dispute resolution mechanism agreed to by the parties in terms of clause 24 of the sub-lease agreement is arbitration.  Further that the matter is
lis pendens
as there is already a similar matter, involving the same parties, the same set of facts and a similar relief pending before this Court in CCT/0001/2023.
[5]     Conversely, counsel for the applicant contends that the applicant has approached this Court in terms of section 4(2) of the Arbitration Act 1980
[1]
to vary the arbitration agreement due to the respondent’s recalcitrant attitude. That the applicant did all he could to have the matter resolved through arbitration but that the respondent is a stumbling block. At any rate, so proceeds the argument, the respondent’s remedy was to apply for stay of proceedings in terms of section 7 of the Act and not to challenge this Court’s jurisdiction to hear the matter.
[6]     Concerning
lis pendens,
counsel contends that CCT/0001/2023 is only focused on damages for breach of the agreement while in the instant matter the applicant is claiming advance rent payment and arrear rentals.
Discussions
[7]     I must first point out that whether arbitration agreement exists between the parties or not needs evidence if it does not appear
ex facie
the pleadings. In
casu
, the point is raised in the Answer. However, the arbitration agreement is contained in the sub-lease agreement which is annexed to the Originating Application in terms of rules 50(1) and 109(5) of High Court Civil Litigation Rules 2024.
[2]
Therefore, it is indisputable that there is arbitration agreement between the parties.
[8]     In the circumstances, a good starting point is to look more closely at the arbitration agreement to determine if the dispute between the parties is arbitrable. Clause 24 of the sub-lease agreement is couched as follows:
“24    DISPUTE RESOLUTION
24.1   Each party consents to the referral to arbitration of any dispute arising out of this agreement, whether in relation to its formation, interpretation, performance, termination or otherwise. The arbitrator shall be a senior counsel who has practised as such at the bar in South Africa and or Lesotho for not less than five years. The arbitration shall be held in English language in the city where the bar at which the arbitrator is located, subject to evidence being given in the chosen language of the witness and translated where this is reasonably necessary. Except to any extent otherwise directed by the arbitrator from time to time, the uniform rules of the high courts, in effect for the time being, shall apply to any such arbitration, and the arbitrator shall, in addition to the powers conferred by legislation, have all the powers of a high court judge to the fullest extent lawfully possible. Any arbitration proceedings pursuant to the arbitration agreement contained in this sub-clause [24.1] shall be treated as confidential by the parties to this agreement.”
[9]     In my respectful view, given the ambit of clause 24, the dispute is arbitrable.  Obviously, the applicant wants the respondent to perform its part of the bargain by paying advance rent and arrear rentals per the agreement.
[10]   Having determined that the dispute is arbitrable, I now turn to consider the preliminary point that this Court does not have jurisdiction to hear the matter or that it should decline jurisdiction given the arbitration agreement between the parties.
[11]   Jurisdiction refers to the court’s competence or power to hear and determine a case.
[3]
The issue of jurisdiction must be interrogated from the onset since it is only when a court has jurisdiction over a matter that it can enter a valid and enforceable judgment. It is trite and requires no authority that the dispute being commercial in nature, is justiciable before this Court considering the amounts involved. Technically, this Court has the necessary competency to hear this matter. However, that is not the end of the matter.
[12]   It is well-recognised that where arbitration agreements exist, courts should be slow to intervene unless the agreements offend public policy. For better appreciation of the discussions that follow, I interpose to quote
in
extenso
section 4 of the Arbitration Act 1980.
“PART III
Effect of arbitration agreements
4.       Binding
effect of arbitration agreements between the parties and the powers of the court in relation thereto.
(1) unless the agreement otherwise provides, an arbitration clause shall not be capable of being terminated except by consent of all the parties thereto.
(2)     The court may at any time on the application of any party to an arbitration agreement on good cause shown;
(a)     set aside the arbitration agreement; or
(b)     order that any particular dispute referred to in arbitration agreement shall not be referred to arbitration; or
(c)      order that the arbitration agreement shall cease to have effect with reference to any dispute referred.”
[13]   It is imperative to refer to the case that – as is the case now before me – was concerned with interpretation of the above section. For that reason, the case of
Bataung Chabeli Constructions (Pty) Ltd v. Road Fund and Others
[4]
is enlightening. At the heart of the appeal in that matter was whether the High Court properly declined to entertain a dispute in the face of arbitration agreement between the parties.
[14]   Having interrogated section 4, Mtshiya AJA had the following to say in delivering the unanimous decision of the Court of Appeal:
“[20] Guided by the parties’ own agreement,
the Act and the persuasive case authorities cited above, I have no hesitation in concluding that by opting for arbitration, as is the case in casu, the parties voluntarily selected a dispute resolution mechanism as an alternative to litigation through the conventional courts. That choice ought to be respected by the courts.  Indeed if a party wishes to abandon an arbitration agreement, it is at liberty to do so as provided for under section 4 (2) of the Act quoted above. That is not the case in casu. The parties are still bound by the arbitration agreement in as far as any disputes arising under the contract are concerned...”
[21]   The foregoing
leads me to the final conclusion that, as long as arbitration remained the declared route of resolving disputes under the contract, it was not proper for the appellant to change course and follow the litigation route through the courts. To that end, the High Court cannot be faulted for having declined jurisdiction. As already pointed out in paragraphs 12 and 13 of this judgment, without jurisdiction, this court cannot consider the merits of the appeal. The High Court, in my view, correctly respected the contractual arrangement between the parties.”
[5]
[15]   Likewise, the Court of appeal reiterated the same message in
Kompi & Others v. Government of the Kingdom of Lesotho & Others
,
[6]
that public policy encourages respect for adherence to agreements to resolve disputes through arbitration instead of recourse to courts of law. The Court repeatedly and strongly emphasised that a party willing to invoke section 4(2) of the Act and resile from an agreement to submit a dispute to arbitration must make out a very strong case for that.
[7]
[16]   Concerning the argument that the respondent was free to seek the remedy of stay in terms of section 7(2) of the Act, the Court of Appeal made it abundantly clear that the remedy of stay did not denude the other party’s right to object to jurisdiction. The Court emphatically stated that there was nothing in sections 4 and 7 or the scheme of the Arbitration Act to suggest that a party to an arbitration agreement may only resist recourse to court in breach of section 4 by relying on the remedy of stay.
[8]
[17]   Similarly, in
Phomolong Investment (Pty) Ltd v. KEL Property Company (Pty) Ltd
[9]
the Court of Appeal traversed the same legal issues regarding the effect of sections 4 and 7 of the Act. It referred to its earlier decisions in
Bataung Chabeli
and
Kompi
,
supra
and arrived at the same conclusion. The Court said the following in overturning the decision of the Court
a quo
refusing to decline jurisdiction:
“[34] In the circumstances of this case, the position taken by the court a quo cannot be correct. The court was dealing with a permissive provision of the law where its discretion, in my view, should have been used in the interest of giving force to the agreement between the parties. Faced with the appellant’s adamant stance that the arbitration agreement be honoured (i.e. through the raising of its special plea), there was nothing to stop the court from ensuring that the arbitration agreement was adhered to. The court should have placed it upon the respondent to establish good cause for proceeding with the court process.
[35]
In taking this position, I derive comfort from the position taken by this court in Kompi, supra. I have reproduced part of that position under paragraph 19 of this judgment. Consequently, and relying on that judgment, I also “see nothing in the language of ss 4 and 7, or indeed the scheme of the Arbitration Act, which suggests that a party to an arbitration agreement may only resist recourse to court in breach of s. 4 by relying on the remedy of stay”. That conclusion destroys the foundation on which the decision of the court a quo stood in refusing to decline jurisdiction as had been prayed for by the appellant."
[10]
[18]   Plainly, our jurisprudence is rich on the issues that arose in this matter. I am profoundly shocked that the applicant is regurgitating the same arguments that were raised and decidedly dealt with by the Court of Appeal in the three judgments. The golden thread running through these judgments is that to maintain the sanctity of arbitration agreements, courts must respect agreements between the parties to resolve their disputes through arbitration and outside of litigation;  that it is only for good cause that a party may be allowed to resile from arbitration agreement; and that a party to arbitration agreement does not necessarily have to invoke the remedy of stay under section 7 of the Act to move the court to decline jurisdiction.
[19]   I have carefully considered the argument that the
“[a]pplicant instituted this matter with the intention to vary from the arbitration, after numerous attempts to have it resolved through arbitration but to no avail.”
[11]
This is an afterthought. Before me is not an application for variation or for the applicant to resile from the arbitration agreement. The reliefs that the applicant is seeking appear in the first page of the Originating Application. None is for variation. Moreover, the argument that the respondent has a recourse under section 7 is also meritless given how the Court of Appeal has interpreted this section in previous decisions.
Disposition
[20]   It bears repeating that the dispute between the parties is arbitrable under clause 24 of the Arbitration Agreement as it relates to performance under the sub-lease agreement. Given jurisprudence developed by the Court of Appeal, which I am bound to follow, the ineluctable conclusion I arrive at is that as long as arbitration remains the declared route of resolving disputes between the applicant and the respondent, this Court must decline jurisdiction to entertain the matter. And I so do.
[21]   Given my decision on the point regarding jurisdiction, it is not necessary that I address the one relating to
lis pendens.
Costs
[22]      The respondent has requested costs on a higher scale arguing that the applicant was fully aware that the matter was arbitrable in terms of their agreement and ought to be dealt as such and not through litigation. Given a plethora of authorities on the subject and how easily accessible they are, there is substance in this argument.
[23]   On the other hand, the issues involved are technical to a lay person. My
prima facie
view is that the attorneys for the applicant and his counsel were grossly negligent. Either they did not familiarize themselves with the law as they should have or they disregarded it. I would have seriously considered awarding costs
de bonis
propriis
against them had the applicant moved in that direction.
[24]   In sum, I am reluctant to award costs on attorney and client scale. Neither am I prepared to award costs
de bonis propriis
where they were not sought and where I did not give the lawyers concerned an opportunity to address me on the issue. In fact, counsel for the applicant simply filed heads of argument and was not in attendance when the matter was argued. Therefore, I award party and party costs.
The order
[25]   I accordingly order as follows:
1. that this court declines jurisdiction to hear the matter in CCT/0010/25.
2. that the applicant shall bear the costs of the matter.
_____________________
MATHABA J
For the Applicant:           Adv. T.E Ts’eole
For the Respondent:        Adv. S. Ts’abeha
